title: Raleigh Pastor Pleads Guilty to Stealing Over $400,000 in COVID Relief Funds
teaser: Mitchell Summerfield, pastor of Word of God Fellowship Church in Raleigh, North Carolina, pleaded guilty on May 27, 2025 to conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud. He fraudulently obtained more than $400,000 in federal COVID-19 relief loans across multiple entities he controlled. He now faces a maximum of 30 years in federal prison.
body:
There is a version of pastoral leadership that involves guiding a congregation through hardship, offering counsel during crisis, and modeling integrity under pressure. Mitchell Summerfield, pastor of Word of God Fellowship Church in Raleigh, North Carolina, appears to have chosen a different path. According to the IRS Criminal Investigation, Summerfield pleaded guilty on May 27, 2025 to conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud. The conduct spanned an entire year, from July 2020 to July 2021, placing it squarely within the most acute phase of pandemic-era financial relief distribution. For a spiritual leader charged with shepherding his flock, the timing is theologically inconvenient.

The federal charges stem from Summerfield's submission of false and fraudulent applications for two distinct categories of COVID-19 relief funding. The programs in question were the Paycheck Protection Program and the Economic Injury Disaster Loan program. Both were designed to channel emergency funds to legitimate businesses and organizations struggling under the weight of the pandemic. Summerfield, according to federal investigators, understood the programs well enough to exploit them across multiple entities he controlled. The total fraudulent amount exceeded $400,000.

The scope of the scheme is worth pausing on. These were not casual errors in paperwork or minor misclassifications of expenses. Summerfield submitted applications that were, in the language of the IRS, false and fraudulent, and he did so through multiple entities. The structure of the fraud, spread across several organizations he controlled, suggests a deliberate architecture rather than an impulsive lapse in judgment. Federal investigators and prosecutors clearly drew the same conclusion.

The charges to which Summerfield pleaded guilty carry significant legal weight. Conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud are federal felonies that together expose Summerfield to a maximum sentence of 30 years in federal prison. That ceiling reflects how seriously Congress and the federal judiciary treat financial crimes that exploit government relief systems and federally insured financial institutions. Entering a guilty plea removes the uncertainty of trial but does not remove the consequences of the conduct itself.

It is worth noting the institutional context here. The IRS Criminal Investigation division has made COVID-19 relief fraud a sustained enforcement priority in the years since the programs closed. Clergy and faith-based organizations were not exempt from application requirements or from scrutiny. The investigation and prosecution of Summerfield reflects a broader federal effort to recover funds diverted from programs that were intended to keep legitimate employers solvent during an unprecedented economic disruption.

The pandemic relief programs were, in many respects, built on trust. The Small Business Administration processed applications rapidly and with limited upfront verification, relying substantially on applicants certifying their own eligibility and the accuracy of their submissions. Summerfield's alleged exploitation of that system is particularly pointed given that religious leaders occupy a social role premised on moral accountability. The irony of a pastor betraying a trust-based system is not subtle, even by the standards of accountability journalism.

Summerfield's conduct also raises questions that extend beyond his personal culpability. A congregation, by its nature, entrusts its spiritual and often practical direction to its pastor. Members of Word of God Fellowship Church presumably held Summerfield in a position of moral authority. The revelation that their leader was simultaneously submitting fraudulent federal loan applications across multiple controlled entities during the height of the pandemic will not be an easy one for that community to absorb.

The year-long timeline of the fraud, July 2020 through July 2021, coincides with a period during which sermons across the country frequently addressed themes of sacrifice, community, honesty, and trust. It is not known what Summerfield preached during those months. What is known is what he was doing in parallel: submitting applications federal investigators have described as false and fraudulent. That contrast between pulpit and practice is, to put it clinically, a data point.

Plea agreements in federal criminal cases typically require defendants to acknowledge the factual basis for the charges as part of the record. By pleading guilty, Summerfield has entered into that legal acknowledgment. The case now moves toward sentencing, where a federal judge will determine the appropriate consequence within the statutory range. With a maximum exposure of 30 years, even a fraction of that range represents a significant reckoning.

COVID-19 relief fraud prosecutions have encompassed a wide range of defendants, from small business owners to sophisticated financial actors. The presence of a pastor among them is not unprecedented, but it does carry a particular resonance. Faith communities were among those hardest hit by the social and economic disruptions of the pandemic, and federal relief programs were in part designed to help sustain those institutions. The allegation that Summerfield exploited those programs for personal and institutional gain adds a layer of institutional betrayal to the legal one.

It is also worth observing what Summerfield's guilty plea does and does not tell us. It confirms that he has admitted, in a federal court, to the conspiracy charges as stated. It does not, at this stage, tell us precisely how the funds were used, what representations were made to his congregation during or after the scheme, or what entities were involved beyond the general reference to multiple organizations he controlled. Those details may emerge at sentencing or in subsequent proceedings. For now, the public record is anchored to a guilty plea and a statutory maximum of three decades.

Mitchell Summerfield's case will join a long federal ledger of COVID-19 relief fraud prosecutions, but his pastoral title gives it a particular clarity as an object lesson. The programs he exploited were funded by taxpayers, including, in all likelihood, members of his own congregation. The faith he was entrusted to represent explicitly condemns dishonesty and the misuse of communal resources. On both counts, the verdict, so to speak, was entered by Summerfield himself on May 27, 2025, in the form of a guilty plea. The sentencing court will determine what comes next.
subjectTitle: Pastor, Word of God Fellowship Church
